coredata.dc:title: Novel red-emitting CDs@LaCaAl3O7:Eu3+ nanocomposites: A sustainable breakthrough for optical thermometry, indoor plant growth and intelligent security labels

coredata.dc:description: A series of melilite LaCaAl3O7:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) nanoparticles (LCAO:xEu3+ NPs) are synthesized using a solution combustion method with spinach extract as a bio-fuel. When carbon dots (CDs) derived from papaya seeds via microwave-assisted synthesis are introduced into LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs, the resulting bright red-emitting CDs(y)@LCAO:7Eu3+ (y = 2, 4, 6, 8 wt%) nanocomposites (NCs) exhibited a wide excitation band peaking at approximately 395 nm, aligning well with the emission range (550–750 nm) of near-ultraviolet (n-UV) LED chips. Remarkably, the CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrated a 20-fold increase in PL intensity compared to LCAO:7Eu3+ alone, attributed to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. This enhancement likely arises from the CDs capturing electrons and transferring energy to Eu3+ ions, offering a simple and eco-friendly strategy to improve luminescent properties. Furthermore, the material retained 91.5 % of its luminescence intensity at 420 K, with an activation energy of 0.39 eV, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. With an internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of 89.58 % and impressive color purity (99.7 %), the composite achieved a relative sensitivity of 3.10 % K−1 at 300 K, highlighting its suitability for non-contact optical thermometry. The material exhibited minimal chromaticity shifts even at high temperatures, ensuring excellent chromaticity stability. A white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs) fabricated using commercial blue and green phosphors combined with CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs on a 395 nm near ultra violet (n-UV) LED chip achieved a color rendering index (CRI) exceeding 90 under varying currents. Additionally, the NPs demonstrated remarkable potential for indoor agriculture, promoting chilli plant growth by increasing height, stem diameter, and leaf width by 6.17 %, 21.42 %, and 10.65 %, respectively, and boosting total chlorophyll content by 19.50 % compared to one grown under natural light. Furthermore, luminescence-based binary and quick response (QR) code encryption showcased its advanced applications in optical security. These findings emphasize the material's versatility for sustainable indoor plant cultivation and anticounterfeiting (AC) technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: To show that the w-LED can improve plant development, its efficacy is evaluated in indoor plant cultivation. w-LED made using CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs and an n-UV 365 nm LED chip. In order to maximize photosynthesis, the produced w-LED probably generates enough blue and red light to overlap with the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll because it emits white light, which is a mixture of wavelengths including blue, green, and red. Using the chilli plant as an example, the outcomes of plant growth studies are compared by adjusting the experimental circumstances. First, two plastic pots filled with soil are used to hold two healthy chilli seedlings that had each germinated independently under sunlight and w-LED light. Throughout the research, same amounts of water and manure will be added to containers 1 and 2. To support photosynthesis throughout the day, plants in containers 1 and 2 receive 12\u201316 h of sunlight and w-LED light, respectively. The plant in container 1 uses the glucose that is saved during the day through photosynthesis as fuel at night. This energy promotes the growth and upkeep of cells. After 12\u201316 h of exposure to w-LED light, the plant in container 2 is subsequently kept in the dark. The lack of light facilitates the plant's energy allocation for cell elongation and division.Seeing the seeds germinated at about day 14, we have recorded the chilli plant growth up to 55 days. Every day between day 15 and day 55, the plants growth in both groups is tracked with practical results shown in Fig. 8a. By day 55, w-LED-grown plants exhibited enhanced growth compared to sunlight-grown plants. The w-LED group showed thicker stems, larger leaves, and denser foliage, demonstrating the light's ability to enhance photosynthetic efficiency. It is found that the w-LED lighting with enough red and blue lights can greatly promote the growth of leaves [96]. Quantitative growth data (Table 9) highlighted the following improvements for w-LED-grown plants compared to sunlight-grown plants: Plant height: 6.17 % increase, Stem diameter: 21.42 % increase, Leaf width: 10.65 % increase (Fig. 8b\u2013d). The ability of plants to use and control light energy is reflected in the content and makeup of photosynthetic pigments, which has a direct impact on the rate at which green plants photosynthesize [97]. One of the photosynthetic pigments, chlorophyll, has the ability to absorb, transmit, and transform light energy. This energy has been recovered by dissociating in an acetone solution. Photosynthetic pigment analysis revealed a significant enhancement in all chlorophyll content (Fig. 8e). The w-LED group showed a 19.50 % increase in total chlorophyll content compared to the sunlight group (Table 10). The combined red and blue wavelengths of the w-LED promoted the synthesis of photosynthetic pigments, enhancing the plant's photosynthetic rate and overall growth. These findings support the application of the fabricated w-LED as an effective tool for indoor cultivation, particularly in environments with limited access to natural sunlight. The artificial lighting system extended the duration of photosynthetic activity, leading to better plant growth and development.The authors (K.M and S.Y.W) want to thank the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) of the Republic of China for the financial support through grant numbers NSTC-112-2112-M-259-017, NSTC-113-2112-M-259-010, NSTC-112-2811-M-259-012 and NSTC-113-2811-M-259-003.

The authors (K.M and S.Y.W) want to thank the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) of the Republic of China for the financial support through grant numbers NSTC-112-2112-M-259-017, NSTC-113-2112-M-259-010, NSTC-112-2811-M-259-012 and NSTC-113-2811-M-259-003.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of melilite LaCaAl3O7:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) nanoparticles (LCAO:xEu3+ NPs) are synthesized using a solution combustion method with spinach extract as a bio-fuel. When carbon dots (CDs) derived from papaya seeds via microwave-assisted synthesis are introduced into LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs, the resulting bright red-emitting CDs(y)@LCAO:7Eu3+ (y = 2, 4, 6, 8 wt%) nanocomposites (NCs) exhibited a wide excitation band peaking at approximately 395 nm, aligning well with the emission range (550–750 nm) of near-ultraviolet (n-UV) LED chips. Remarkably, the CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrated a 20-fold increase in PL intensity compared to LCAO:7Eu3+ alone, attributed to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. This enhancement likely arises from the CDs capturing electrons and transferring energy to Eu3+ ions, offering a simple and eco-friendly strategy to improve luminescent properties. Furthermore, the material retained 91.5 % of its luminescence intensity at 420 K, with an activation energy of 0.39 eV, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. With an internal quantum efficiency (IQE) of 89.58 % and impressive color purity (99.7 %), the composite achieved a relative sensitivity of 3.10 % K−1 at 300 K, highlighting its suitability for non-contact optical thermometry. The material exhibited minimal chromaticity shifts even at high temperatures, ensuring excellent chromaticity stability. A white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs) fabricated using commercial blue and green phosphors combined with CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs on a 395 nm near ultra violet (n-UV) LED chip achieved a color rendering index (CRI) exceeding 90 under varying currents. Additionally, the NPs demonstrated remarkable potential for indoor agriculture, promoting chilli plant growth by increasing height, stem diameter, and leaf width by 6.17 %, 21.42 %, and 10.65 %, respectively, and boosting total chlorophyll content by 19.50 % compared to one grown under natural light. Furthermore, luminescence-based binary and quick response (QR) code encryption showcased its advanced applications in optical security. These findings emphasize the material's versatility for sustainable indoor plant cultivation and anticounterfeiting (AC) technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Indoor plant cultivation has gained significant attention for fostering plant growth within controlled environments. These conditions include optimized air quality, suitable radiance, temperature control, and other specialized growth parameters. Such an approach safeguards plants from extreme weather events like severe droughts and frequent flooding. By enabling precise manipulation of growth factors, indoor cultivation improves crop yield and quality, addressing global food scarcity to some extent. Plants rely on light energy not only for photosynthesis but also as a signal to adapt growth patterns, influencing processes such as flowering, seed germination, seedling development, and responses to competition from neighbouring plants. The light environment comprising intensity, luminosity, and spectral composition—is particularly vital for indoor cultivation. Blue (400–500 nm), red (620–690 nm), and far-red (700–780 nm) light are indispensable for processes such as photosynthesis, phototropism, and photomorphogenesis. These wavelengths align with the absorption spectra of primary photosynthetic pigments like chlorophyll and carotenoids. Moreover, red and far-red light are predominantly absorbed by the photoreceptor phytochrome protein, which regulates plant germination, growth, and maturation through its inactive (PR) and active (PFR) states [1–4].

From the ancient use of fire to the advent of fluorescent lamps, humanity has pursued efficient lighting solutions. Phosphor-converted white light-emitting diodes (pc-w-LEDs) represent the fourth generation of light sources, offering exceptional luminous efficiency, high color rendering, and longevity [5]. The performance of pc-w-LEDs is significantly influenced by the luminescent properties of phosphors, especially red-emitting phosphors that enhance color rendering and improve device performance. Red light is also crucial for promoting plant growth, making pc-w-LEDs an excellent light source for indoor agriculture due to their spectral alignment with plant photoreceptors. Compared to traditional light sources such as high-pressure sodium, metal halide, and incandescent lamps, pc-w-LEDs offer tunable spectral distributions, energy efficiency, and reduced pesticide reliance, contributing to sustainable agriculture and low-carbon production. Developing red phosphors with high purity, thermal stability, and luminescence efficiency is essential for advancing pc-w-LEDs for plant cultivation applications.

Phosphors typically consist of a host material doped with activator ions capable of emitting light. Red-emitting activators include both transition metal ions (e.g., Fe3+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+/Mn4+) and rare earth ions (e.g., Sm3+, Pr3+, Eu2+/Eu3+). Rare earth-doped melilite compounds have recently attracted attention for their ability to incorporate high concentrations of activators and facilitate energy transfer (ET), enhancing luminescence and reducing costs [6]. The melilite structure of CaLnAl3O7 materials makes them valuable for optical applications, with compounds like CaYAl3O7 (CYAM) and CaGdAl3O7 (CGAM) showing promise in long-lasting phosphorescence (LLP) [7–11]. It is thus seen from the literature survey (Table 1 ) [12–16] that there are not many studies done on LaCaAl3O7 as host. Despite significant advancements, detailed investigations of LaCaAl3O7 (LCAO) doped with Eu3+ and the associated ET mechanisms remain sparse. This study addresses this gap by exploring the structural and luminescence properties of LCAO:Eu3+ synthesized via a solution combustion method using spinach extract as a green fuel.

Lanthanide-doped nanomaterials often suffer from surface defects, narrow absorption bands, and reduced luminescence quantum efficiency due to nanoscale surface effects [17]. Strategies to mitigate these challenges include optimizing synthesis techniques, incorporating sensitizers, and leveraging energy transfer processes. Quantum carbon dots (CDs), known for their low toxicity, high reliability, and luminescent properties, have shown great potential for enhancing phosphor performance. Based on recent research, adding CDs to the phosphor results in notable increases in photoluminescence (PL) intensity using nanocomposite (NCs) materials. Notably, when N-doped CDs are inserted into Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphors, an extraordinary 342.82-fold increase in PL intensity is noted [18]. In particular, as compared to their counterparts without encapsulated CDs, (YGO):Eu3+@AVCDs (5 wt%) and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ NCs showed a 4-fold and 66.23-fold increase in PL intensity, respectively [19,20]. Additionally, the emission intensity of CDs@MgWO4:Tb3+ NCs is 7 times higher than that of MgWO4:Tb3+ phosphor [21]. The FRET from CDs to Eu3+ increased the PL intensity in YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+, Tb3+/CDs NCs, according to some research on composites of CDs with RE-doped luminous materials [22]. Furthermore, compared to YBO3:Eu3+, the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ displayed better PL intensity [23]. Despite these advances, no prior research has examined CDs integrated with LCAO:Eu3+ nanophosphors. This study synthesizes CDs from papaya seeds and fabricates unique red-emitting CDs@LCAO:Eu3+ NCs to enhance the performance of LCAO:Eu3+. The resulting NCs exhibit promising applications in optical thermometry, solid-state lighting, and anti-counterfeiting.

Counterfeiting, encompassing counterfeit goods, pharmaceuticals, art, and currency, remains a pressing global concern, with counterfeit market has grown significantly, reaching a huge value of $206 billion by 2021 and growing at a noteworthy pace of 14.0 % [24,25]. Advanced anti-counterfeiting (AC) technologies are thus essential. Luminescent materials have emerged as promising tools for AC, offering customizable, multicolored patterns for encryption. However, conventional methods often suffer from structural imperfections and limited durability [26]. To address these limitations, this work utilizes CDs@LCAO:Eu3+ as luminescent inks for data encoding via binary and QR codes, combining fluorescence and UV-triggered encryption to achieve superior security.

The novelty of this study lies in the innovative synthesis of LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) phosphors using a cost-effective combustion method and the integration of CDs into these phosphors. Leveraging green precursors like spinach extract and papaya seeds, the synthesized CDs@LCAO:Eu3+ NCs demonstrate significant PL enhancements, driven by mechanisms such as FRET. Further, it is possible to employ the CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ for optical thermometry applications because of their high Ea value of 0.39 eV. Previous studies covered the use of rare earth doped phosphors with CDs for either anticounterfeiting or indoor plant growing applications using fabricated pc-w-LED. But few have tackled both issues at once. This dual-purpose material addresses the needs of both indoor agriculture and anti-counterfeiting, outperforming conventional phosphors in terms of efficiency and versatility.

Prior research has mostly concentrated on broad increases in light output and efficiency, frequently ignoring the fine tweaking needed for various plant species and growth phases. By creating and describing unique CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs that are specifically designed to emit red wavelengths—which are crucial for photosynthesis and photomorphogenesis—our study overcomes these constraints. By precisely tuning red emission wavelengths critical for plant growth, this work optimizes w-LED grow lights to enhance chilli plant growth, achieving increases in height, stem diameter, and leaf width by 6.17 %, 21.42 %, and 10.65 %, respectively, alongside a 19.50 % rise in chlorophyll content. The integration of CDs into LCAO:Eu3+ also enables advanced AC applications, demonstrating superior encryption capabilities through luminescent binary codes and covert QR codes. These findings highlight the potential of CDs@LCAO:Eu3+ NCs for sustainable agriculture and next-generation optical security solutions.

The creative use of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ and dual-emissive CDs for intelligent AC labels, filling important research gaps. In contrast to conventional techniques, this work uses a dual-layer encryption strategy that combines secret QR codes and luminous binary codes that are only visible when exposed to UV light. High-precision QR codes combined with luminescence-based binary decoding provide improved security by bridging the gap between UV-triggered complexity and visible simplicity. Furthermore, the luminous materials allow for the encoding of hidden information without sacrificing appearance in regular lighting. By combining fluorescence, data encryption, and optical security in a unique way, this method opens the door for next-generation optical display technologies, secure packaging, and sophisticated anti-counterfeiting methods.

The materials required for nanomaterial synthesis are obtained from Sigma Aldrich, India. These included lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate [La(NO3)3·6H2O], calcium nitrate tetrahydrate [Ca(NO3)2·4H2O], aluminum nitrate nonahydrate [Al(NO3)3·9H2O], europium nitrate pentahydrate [Eu(NO3)3·5H2O], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), ethanol, and sodium dodecyl sulfonate. Additional chemicals such as phosphoric acid (H3PO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) with 99.99 % purity are used. All reagents are of analytical grade and utilized without further modification. Deionized (DI) water is employed for preparing solutions. Spinach leaves, chili seeds, and papaya seeds are sourced from a local supermarket in Tumkur, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of pure LCAO and LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Spinach extract preparation: Fresh spinach leaves are thoroughly washed under running water to remove contaminants, dirt, and pesticides. The leaves are dried using a clean cloth, chopped into smaller pieces, and blended with distilled water (100–200 mL for approximately 50 g of leaves) to form a paste. This mixture is filtered through cheesecloth to extract the liquid, which is collected and stored in an airtight container under refrigeration. The preparation process is shown schematically in Scheme 1 a.

Synthesis of pure and doped LCAO:xEu
3+
NPs: Calculated amounts of La(NO3)3, Ca(NO3)2, and Al(NO3)3 are dissolved in DI water to prepare a homogeneous solution. For doped samples, part of La(NO3)3 is replaced with Eu(NO3)3 in the required stoichiometric ratios. Spinach extract, acting as both a stabilizer and fuel, is added to maintain a fuel-to-oxidizer (F/O) ratio of 1:1. The mixture is stirred continuously at room temperature for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer to ensure homogeneity. Gentle heating is applied during stirring to reduce water content, resulting in a viscous gel. The gel is transferred to a ceramic crucible and heated in a preheated muffle furnace at 500 °C. During combustion, gases such as CO2, H2O, and N2 are released, producing a lightweight, foam-like product. The as-synthesized powder is then calcined at 800 °C for 4 h to enhance crystallinity and phase purity. After natural cooling, the powder is ground into a fine product. Scheme 1b illustrates the synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs using papaya seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Papaya seeds are first dried under sunlight to remove moisture and then ground into a fine powder using a blender. The powder is mixed with phosphoric acid (H3PO4) to form a precursor solution, which is subjected to microwave irradiation for 5 min to induce carbonization and CDs formation. The solution is then filtered using a sterile PTFE syringe filter (0.22 μm) to remove unreacted materials and larger impurities. Dialysis with a 1 kDa dialysis bag is employed to eliminate smaller impurities and byproducts. The purified CDs solution is collected and stored under refrigeration for subsequent experiments. The process is schematically depicted in Scheme 1c.

The synthesis of CDs@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs involved accurately weighing LCAO:7Eu3+ powder and calculating the required amount of CDs for the desired wt %. Based on the concentration of the CDs solution, the appropriate volume is mixed with LCAO:7Eu3+ powder in a glass beaker, with DI water added if the mixture became too viscous. Sonication is performed using a probe sonicator (20–40 kHz, 100–200 W) for 30 min to ensure uniform CDs distribution on the LCAO:7Eu3+ surface. The mixture is then dried in a hot-air oven at 60–80 °C until all solvent evaporated, typically within 24 h. The dried composite is further heated at 200–300 °C in a furnace for 3 h to enhance interactions between the CDs and LCAO:7Eu3+. Finally, the composite is ground into a fine powder. This procedure is repeated for CDs weight percentages of 2, 4, 6, and 8 wt%, adjusting the CDs solution volume for each case. The synthesis process is outlined schematically in Scheme 1d.

To prepare security ink, a solution is formulated by combining deionized (DI) water (83 %), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 1 % w/w) for pH adjustment, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a binder. Sodium dodecyl sulfonate (3 mg/L) is added to regulate surface tension, and the mixture is mechanically stirred for 45 min to ensure complete dissolution. Subsequently, 0.5 g of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are incorporated, followed by continuous stirring for 30 min and ultrasonic treatment at 25 kHz for 60 min to achieve uniform dispersion. A vacuum pump is then used to degas the solution for 10 min, effectively removing air bubbles and resulting in a transparent ink ready for immediate application. The fabrication process for the security ink and AC labels, including screen printing and brush application methods, is depicted schematically in Scheme 1e. When applied, the luminous ink emits bright red light at 365 nm under UV exposure, enabling high-resolution imaging via camera.

The fabrication of a white light-emitting diode (w-LED) utilized a near-ultraviolet (n-UV) chip (λ = 395 nm) as the excitation source along with the red-emitting CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs developed in this study. Additionally, green ((Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+) and blue (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+) phosphors are blended with the red CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs in an optimized 8:1:3 wt ratio for balanced white light emission. The phosphors are mixed thoroughly to ensure homogeneity and incorporated into a transparent silicone resin. This mixture is evenly applied to the n-UV chip to create a uniform phosphor layer capable of converting n-UV light to visible light via photoluminescence. The coated chip is then cured under controlled conditions to secure the phosphor layer. Electroluminescence properties of the fabricated w-LED are evaluated using a USB 4000 spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA), which recorded the electroluminescence spectrum and allowed analysis of parameters such as luminous intensity, color rendering index (CRI), correlated color temperature (CCT), and luminous efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Germination of chili seeds before planting
ce:section.ce:para: Chili seeds are pre-germinated by soaking in lukewarm water for 24 h to enhance germination. Two trays are prepared with well-draining garden soil. Five soaked seeds are planted in each tray, placed approximately 1 cm deep, and covered with a thin layer of soil. The soil is gently watered to maintain moisture without waterlogging.
Sunlight Set: Trays are placed in a location with 8–12 h of direct sunlight daily.
w-LED Set: Trays are placed under a w-LED light, keeping the light 4–6 inches above the soil surface and providing 16 h of light per day.

The temperature is maintained at 25–30 °C, and the soil is kept moist but not overly wet. Germination began after 14 days, with seedlings emerging. Once seedlings developed 2–4 leaves, they are transplanted into larger pots for continued growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of chlorophyll content in chili plant leaves using acetone
ce:section.ce:para:
Chlorophyll content is estimated following the method described by Harborne [27]. Approximately 0.1 g of freshly chopped leaves are ground in 10 mL of 80 % acetone. The homogenate is centrifuged at 5000–10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant is collected. The residue is re-extracted until it became colourless. Absorbance measurements of the solution are taken at 480, 645, and 663 nm against acetone as a blank. Chlorophyll concentrations are calculated using the formulas provided by Arnon [28]:

To study chemical composition, charge state, and binding energy
Horiba spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon light source emitting at 430 nm

ce:section.ce:para:
The structural characteristics of the synthesized materials are analyzed using XRD. The diffraction patterns of pure LCAO and LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) are presented in Fig. 1 a, confirming their highly crystalline nature. The diffraction peaks correspond to the (110), (001), (200), (111), (210), (201), (211), (220), (002), (310), (102), (301), (311), (202), (212), (400), (410), (330), (312), (331), (213), (512), (104), and (522) planes of the tetragonal lattice structure of LCAO and doped LCAO:xEu3+. These peaks are observed at specific 2θ values, ranging from 16.03⁰ to 74.51⁰, as indexed against ICDD 38–1251 [12]. This indicates the absence of impurities and confirms the successful incorporation of Eu³⁺ ions into the host lattice. The tetragonal structure crystallizes in the P
4 ‾
2
1
m space group. A magnified view of the XRD patterns in the 2θ range of 30.5–31.5⁰ is shown in Fig. 1b, where a shift in peak positions towards higher angles is observed, due to changes in lattice strain [29]. This shift is attributed to the substitution of larger La3+ ions (r = 1.032 Å, CN = 6) with smaller Eu3+ ions (r = 0.947 Å, CN = 6), resulting in a decrease in interplanar spacing and an increase in diffraction angle (θ). However, to precisely establish the replacement phenomenon, the radius percentage difference (Dr) between the host cations and doped cations are calculated using the following defined formula [30]:
(4)

Here, Rd (CN) represents the effective ionic radius of the dopant ions, while Rs (CN) denotes the effective ionic radius of the potential substitute ions, with CN referring to the coordination number of the respective ions. Since the calculated Dr value between Eu3+ and La3+ is 8.23 %, Eu3+ can successfully replace La³⁺ within the host framework. According to relevant literature, effective substitution typically requires a Dr value below 30 %. As the computed Dr value for Eu3+ and La3+ is significantly lower than this threshold, Eu3+ ions can effectively occupy La3+ sites in the host matrix. However, Eu3+ cannot substitute Ca2+ due to their differing charge states. The XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs (Fig. 1c) reveals a broad peak at 2θ = 19.37⁰, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, and a weaker peak at 2θ = 40.67⁰, attributed to the (100) plane [31]. In CDs(y)@LCAO:7Eu3+ (y = 2, 4, 6, 8 wt%), distinct peaks characteristic of both LCAO and CDs are observed concurrently.

The phase structures of undoped and doped LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) NPs are refined using GSAS II software [32]. The Rietveld refinement patterns for pure LCAO and LCAO:Eu3+ (1, 3, 5, 9, and 11 mol%) are shown in Fig. S1(a–f), with data summarized in Table S1. The refinement pattern for LCAO:7Eu3+ is presented in Fig. 1d, with a high level of agreement between observed (Yobs) and calculated (Ycal) patterns, as indicated by low Rwp (8.61 %), Rp (8.93 %), and χ2 (1.52) values. The crystallographic parameters are well within the 10 % tolerance, and the pseudo-Voigt function is used to improve profile fitting. The 3D crystal structure and coordination environment of the cationic sites in LCAO:Eu3+ are visualized using Vesta 3.5.8 software [33] (Fig. 1e), illustrating a well-ordered network of polyhedra linked by shared edges and corners.

Crystallite size and strain contributions can be evaluated using many models like Debye-Scherrer, Williamson-Hall (W–H), and Size-Strain Plot (SSP) etc. The W–H method accounts for both size and strain-induced broadening, while the SSP approach focuses on the peak profile as a combination of Lorentzian and Gaussian functions [34,35]. These values, along with other structural parameters, are summarized in Table S2, highlighting the superior crystallinity and minimal defects of LCAO:xEu3+ NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis via FE-SEM, EDAX and TEM
ce:section.ce:para: Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) images, shown in Fig. S2(a–g), illustrate the morphology of undoped and Eu3+ (1–11 mol%) doped LCAO NPs synthesized via the solution combustion method. The results reveal a distinct flaky structure for all samples. The nanocrystalline flakes appear flat and irregularly shaped across the micrographs. For undoped LCAO (Fig. S2a), a layered morphology is observed, likely resulting from smooth combustion facilitated by the spinach extract, which ensures the uniform formation of particles. As the Eu3+ concentration increases from 1 to 11 mol%, the morphology evolves from compact and smooth flakes to more granular and aggregated structures, indicating changes in crystallite growth and particle interactions induced by doping (Figs. S2b–g). At the optimal doping level of 7 mol% (Fig. S2e), the structure exhibits a more defined and uniform layered appearance, suggesting enhanced structural homogeneity at this concentration. The use of spinach extract as a green fuel likely contributes to this uniformity by promoting even combustion.

The standalone CDs synthesized from papaya seeds display a spherical morphology with uniform particle size distribution (Fig. S2h). This morphology can be attributed to the efficient carbonization process, where the organic content of papaya seeds aids in forming well-defined carbon structures with minimal aggregation. FE-SEM images of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (Fig. S2i) show the integration of spherical CDs within the LCAO matrix, as highlighted by the dotted regions. The incorporation of these CDs into the doped LCAO framework is facilitated by their surface chemistry, enabling better interaction and dispersion. Functional groups present in papaya seeds may enhance adhesion and integration, while the natural surfactant properties of spinach extract help minimize particle aggregation, ensuring homogeneous distribution within the composite matrix.

Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) is employed to investigate the elemental composition of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The EDAX spectra and corresponding elemental mappings are shown in Fig. S3. For LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs (Fig. S3a), the spectrum indicates a higher concentration of oxygen, followed by lanthanum, calcium, aluminum, and europium. The inset table provides the weight and atomic percentages of each element. The EDAX spectrum of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (Fig. S3b) shows a small but significant presence of carbon, evidenced by prominent peaks corresponding to carbon components integrated into the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix. This confirms the successful incorporation of CDs. The elemental mappings of lanthanum (purple), calcium (green), aluminum (red), oxygen (blue), europium (white), and carbon (pale blue) for both samples are presented in Fig. S3(c–g) for LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and Fig. S3(h–m) for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs. These maps illustrate the uniform distribution of elements within the respective matrices. The variation in atomic percentages for each element is summarized in the statistical bar graph shown in Fig. S3m, highlighting differences between the undoped and doped samples. These results underscore the successful synthesis and compositional uniformity of the prepared materials.

To gain a deeper understanding of the morphology, size, and structural properties of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs, TEM analysis is conducted. The TEM micrograph of CDs (Fig. 2 b) reveals uniform, spherical particles with no significant agglomeration, confirming efficient synthesis. In contrast, LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs exhibit a flaky morphology (Fig. 2a), consistent with observations from FE-SEM studies. For CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 2c), the CDs are well-dispersed on the flat and flaky LCAO:7Eu3+ surfaces, indicating successful integration of CDs into the host matrix. The HR-TEM image of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs (Fig. 2d) reveals distinct lattice fringes, characteristic of highly crystalline regions. The measured interplanar spacing of 0.507 nm corresponds to the (001) plane of the LCAO:7Eu3+ crystal structure (Fig. 2g). The pixel profile, generated using GATAN Digital Micrograph software [39], confirms the uniformity of lattice fringes with a calculated interplanar distance of d = 0.507 nm (Fig. 2k), in agreement with the measured value, further validating the material's crystallinity. For CDs, the HR-TEM image (Fig. 2e) highlights their nanoscale structure and crystalline nature. The interplanar spacing of 0.306 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2h), a hallmark of CDs [40]. The periodic fringes in the pixel profile confirm consistent lattice spacing with d = 0.350 nm, indicative of well-defined crystalline regions (Fig. 2l). The HR-TEM image of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 2f) demonstrates the successful integration of CDs into the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix. The lattice fringes display interplanar distances of 0.509 nm for LCAO:7Eu3+ and 0.352 nm for CDs, confirming the coexistence of both components (Fig. 2i and j). Pixel profile plots further validate the effective incorporation of CDs into the composite material (Fig. 2m & n). The SAED patterns of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are shown in Fig. 2o and p, respectively. For LCAO:7Eu3+, the SAED pattern features dotted rings corresponding to specific crystallographic planes, emphasizing the material's polycrystalline nature. Similarly, the SAED pattern for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs displays diffraction rings attributed to contributions from both the LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs components. The bright, distinct dots in the SAED patterns further confirm the polycrystalline characteristics of both samples, showcasing successful integration of CDs into the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface composition and oxidation states: XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is employed to study the surface elemental composition and oxidation states of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs. The survey scan XPS spectra for both samples are presented in Fig. S4a, revealing the presence of key elements. In the high-resolution spectrum of La 3d for LCAO:7Eu3+ (Fig. S4b), characteristic peaks are observed at binding energies of 834.12 and 838.40 eV (La 3d5/2) and 850.95 and 854.86 eV (La 3d3/2), indicating the presence of La in the +3 oxidation state. Similarly, the Ca 2p spectrum (Fig. S4c) shows peaks at 350.64 eV (2p1/2) and 347.11 eV (2p3/2), confirming Ca in the +2 oxidation state [41]. The Al 2p spectrum (Fig. S4d) features a single peak at 74.55 eV, attributed to Al in Al(OH)3 form [42]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S4e) reveals four deconvoluted peaks corresponding to –OH (531.78 eV), C–O (532.76 eV), –COOH (533.64 eV), and H2O (534.36 eV), indicating oxygen species within the LCAO:7Eu3+ lattice [43]. Peaks at 1164.13 eV (3d3/2) and 1134.19 eV (3d5/2) in the Eu 3d spectrum (Fig. S4f) confirm the +3 oxidation state of Eu ions. The C 1s spectrum for LCAO:7Eu3+ (Fig. S4g) shows low-intensity peaks at 284.88 and 288.81 eV, likely originating from residual carbon from the stabilizing agents used during synthesis. In CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs, slight shifts in the binding energies of La 3d, Ca 2p, Al 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are observed (Fig. S4h-l, n), suggesting that the introduction of CDs affects the charge distribution and electronic interactions in the composite. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs reveals additional peaks at 284.32 eV (C–C), 284.65 eV (CC), 285.59 eV (C–O–C), and 288.08 eV (-COO), representing various carbon environments introduced by the CDs [43]. Furthermore, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4m) exhibits a peak at 399.69 eV, attributed to pyridinic nitrogen (C–NC), confirming the incorporation of nitrogen-containing functional groups from the CDs [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and compositional analysis: FT-IR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra for LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(y wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ (y = 2, 4, 6, 8 wt%) are shown in Fig. S5. The pristine LCAO:7Eu3+ spectrum exhibits characteristic peaks at 751.8 cm−1 and 600.2 cm−1, corresponding to metal-oxygen (La–O, Ca–O, and Al–O) stretching vibrations, confirming the structural integrity of the LCAO framework. Upon the incorporation of CDs, new peaks emerge and shifts are observed. Peaks at 1670.1 cm−1 and 1567.3 cm−1 are attributed to CO and CC stretching modes, respectively, indicating the presence of functional groups on the surface of the CDs [45]. The peak at 1420.2 cm−1 is associated with C–H bending vibrations, further confirming the integration of CDs. The progressive changes in transmittance intensity with increasing CDs concentration suggest successful incorporation of CDs into the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix. These modifications likely alter the local environment and introduce new functional groups, which enhance optical properties such as luminescence. The energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ions facilitated by these functional groups can significantly improve the material's performance in optical applications, including sensors and display technologies.

The photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of LCAO:7Eu3+ at ambient temperature are shown in Fig. 3 a and b, covering a wavelength range of 200–750 nm. The excitation spectrum (Fig. 3a) is recorded at an emission wavelength of 613 nm, highlighting two distinct regions. The broad charge transfer band (200–350 nm) arises from electronic transitions between the oxygen ions' 2p orbital and the partially filled 4f orbital of Eu3+ ions within the host matrix. Between 350 and 550 nm, several sharp peaks are observed, with a particularly notable peak at 395 nm, corresponding to the 4f-4f transitions of Eu3+ ions. These peaks correspond to transitions from the ground state 7F0 to excited states 5D4, 5L7, 5L6, 5D3, 5D2 and 5D1 at 362, 381, 395, 413, 464 and 531 nm, respectively. The emission spectrum (Fig. 3b) spans 550–750 nm, measured using a 395 nm excitation wavelength. The visible spectrum includes emission bands at 591 nm, 613 nm, 650 nm, and 692 nm, corresponding to transitions from the excited 5D0 state to ground states 7F1, 7F2, 7F3, and 7F4, respectively. Notably, the transition at 591 nm (5D0 state to the 7F1 state) is a magnetic dipole (MD) transition, governed by the selection rule ΔJ = ±1, while the 613 nm (5D0 state to the 7F2 state) transition is an electric dipole (ED) transition, governed by ΔJ = ±2. The 613 nm peak exhibits a vivid red emission, characteristic of hypersensitive ED transitions. Additional peaks at 650 nm (5D0→7F3) and 692 nm (5D0→7F4) align with previously reported data [46]. The hypersensitive ED transitions dominate the emission spectrum, suggesting that Eu3+ ions occupy sites with reduced symmetry within the host lattice. The observed splitting in the emission peaks further confirms the high crystallinity of the materials synthesized via the combustion method.

The nephelauxetic effect is analyzed to assess the reduction in electronic repulsions and the strength of the ligand field formed by the rare earth–oxygen interaction in the LCAO host matrix. The nephelauxetic ratio (β) is calculated using the equation [47]:

where the number of bands detected in the LCAO matrix is denoted by N, the wavenumber of the Eu3+ transition in the host matrix is represented by complex, and the wavenumber of the Eu3+ transition in aqua-ion is shown by aqua−ion environment [48]. The bonding parameter (δ) is used to evaluate the covalent or ionic nature of the bond between Eu3+ and O2− ions:

The measured wavenumber shifts, shown in Table 2 , indicate an overlap between the 2p orbital of oxygen and the 4f orbital of Eu3+. A positive value for δ signifies covalent bonding between Eu3+ and O2− ions, as observed in this study [49]. The analysis confirms that the Eu3+ ions in the LCAO host matrix are bound covalently to oxygen ligands.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of LCAO:Eu3+ at varying Eu3+ concentrations (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 mol%) are shown in Fig. 3c. The spectra, measured in the 550–750 nm range under 395 nm excitation, exhibit consistent peak patterns across all concentrations, with variations in intensity. The most intense emission peak, centered at 613 nm, corresponds to the 5D0→7F2 ED transition of Eu3+ ions. The emission intensity reaches its maximum at 7 mol% Eu3+ concentration. Beyond this concentration, a decline in intensity is observed due to concentration quenching [50]. The inset of Fig. 3c illustrates the trend in 613 nm emission intensity with increasing Eu3+ concentration. The energy transfer mechanisms between dopant ions are evaluated by determining the critical distance (Rc) using the Blasse theory [51]:
(11)
R c
= 2
(
3 V
4
π χ
c
N
)
1 3
where V is the unit cell volume (322.69 Å3), N is the number of cations per unit cell (5), and χc is the optimal dopant concentration (0.07 for Eu3+ doped LCAO). The calculated critical distance Rc = 12.07 Å exceeds 5 Å, indicating that energy transfer between Eu3+ ions occurs via multipolar interaction rather than exchange interaction [52].

Reisfeld's approximation and Dexter's energy transfer formula are used to analyze the specific multipolar interaction responsible for energy transfer [53]:
(12)
I X
= K
[
1 + β
( X )
Q 3
]
− 1
Where the variable X represents the concentration of the activator ion (Eu3+ ions) above the optimal molar concentration, I/X represents the emission intensity per concentration of the activator ion, β and K are constants, and Q can take values such as 3, 6, 8, and 10, which correspond to exchange interaction, dipole–dipole interaction, dipole-quadrupole interaction and quadrupole–quadrupole interaction, respectively.

In Fig. 3d, the logarithm of X is plotted against the logarithm of I/X for the 613 nm emission. The linear fit has a slope of −1.92, yielding Q = 5.76, which approximates to 6. This result indicates that the concentration quenching observed in LCAO:Eu3+ phosphors is dominated by dipole–dipole interactions. The dominance of dipole–dipole interactions in energy transfer suggests a high level of interaction efficiency within the host matrix. The presence of multipolar interactions aligns with the extended critical distance, which facilitates effective energy migration between Eu3+ ions without significant non-radiative losses. This behavior enhances the luminescence performance at optimal dopant concentrations while minimizing losses due to quenching at higher concentrations.

The normalized excitation and emission spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 3e. The excitation spectrum exhibits a broad band centered at 338 nm, attributed to the n-π∗ transitions of CO bonds in the CDs [54]. Upon excitation at 338 nm, the CDs emit a blue fluorescence band centered at 407 nm. This PL behavior is explained by two key phenomena: (1) size variation within the CDs and (2) the distribution of surface energy traps. These factors enable fluorescence through conjugated π-domain band gap transitions and emissions from surface defects. Surface functionalization with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups improves the solubility of CDs in aqueous solutions, offering superior PL properties compared to graphene quantum dots, which are less soluble in common solvents [55]. The excitation spectra of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ and LCAO:7Eu3+ are displayed in Fig. 3f. The sharp peaks observed in the PLE spectrum of LCAO:7Eu3+ correspond to Eu3+ intra-configurational f-f transitions at 613 nm emission, characteristic of the Eu3+ ion. CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit an additional broad band between 200 and 350 nm, attributed to π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of CDs [22,56]. This broad band indicates efficient energy transfer from CDs to Eu³⁺ ions, enhancing the excitation peaks of the nanocomposite compared to the bare LCAO:7Eu3+. The PL spectra of LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs under 395 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 3g. Eu3+ transitions dominate at 591 nm (5D0→7F1), 613 nm (5D0→7F2), 650 nm (5D0→7F3), and 692 nm (5D0→7F4). The overlap between the emission band of CDs and the absorption peaks of Eu³⁺ ions at 362, 381, 395, 413, 464, and 531 nm (Fig. 3h) confirms energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ ions. The emission intensity of CDs(y wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (y = 2, 4, 6, 8) increased 9.4-, 20-, 17.5-, and 13.1-fold, respectively, compared to LCAO:7Eu3+, indicating an optimal CDs concentration of 4 wt% (Fig. 3i). Table 3 lists the comparative data of enhanced PL of different CDs nanocomposite materials [21,57–60]. The enhanced PL intensity is further supported by improved crystallinity, as evidenced by changes in FWHM, attributed to fewer structural imperfections due to the inclusion of CDs.

The significant enhancement in PL intensity for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs is attributed to FRET between CDs (donor) and Eu3+ ions (acceptor). The conditions for FRET include:
1.
Proximity between donor and acceptor (1–10 nm): Confirmed by FE-SEM (Fig. S2i) and TEM (Fig. 2c), which show CDs adhering tightly to the surface of LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs.
2.
Spectral overlap: The emission spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Eu3+ ions (Fig. 3h).
3.
Parallel dipole orientations: FRET occurs via dipole–dipole interactions between transition dipoles of donor and acceptor molecules [61].

When excited at 395 nm, CDs are elevated to an excited state, facilitating energy transfer to Eu3+ ions through non-radiative FRET. This mechanism enables the electrons in Eu3+ to transition from the 7F0 ground state to higher energy levels while electrons in CDs transitioned from HOMO to LUMO with the aid of absorbed photon energy, and eventually relaxing to the 5D0→7F1−4 levels, resulting in bright red emissions (Fig. 4 a) [60]. CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit a broad excitation range (200–550 nm) and a prominent excitation peak at 395 nm, which aligns with the emission wavelengths of commercially popular LED chips (550–750 nm). This compatibility expands the potential applications of these NCs in luminescent materials, including LED lighting and display technologies. Enhanced PL properties, efficient energy transfer, and compatibility with existing technologies make these materials promising candidates for advanced photonic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photometric studies: CIE coordinates and CCT
ce:section.ce:para: The CIE coordinates and correlated color temperature (CCT) of the phosphor samples are analyzed to evaluate their photometric properties. The CIE chromaticity coordinates for Eu3+- doped LCAO at 7 mol% are determined as (0.628, 0.360) using CIE coordinate calculator software [62] as shown in Fig. 4b. Similarly, the CIE coordinates for CDs(y wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (y = 2, 4, 6, 8) are calculated from their PL spectra under 395 nm excitation, and the results are summarized in Table 4 . The CIE diagram for CDs(y wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs is shown in Fig. 4d, with the optimal CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibiting vivid red emission under 365 nm UV light. Its coordinates, (0.699, 0.300), fall within the bright red region, closely matching the NTSC standard (0.67, 0.33) for red phosphors [63]. These results suggest potential application in solid-state display technologies as a red-emitting phosphor.

The CCT values provide insights into the type of light emitted. McCamy's empirical formula [64] is used to calculate CCT values:
(14)
CCT = ‐

(

)
+ 3525

Where, n =
x −

The CCT values are calculated based on color coordinates (x = 0.628, y = 0.360) for LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs, (x = 0.699, y = 0.300) for CDs(4 wt %)@ LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs and the color epicenter of chromaticity (xe = 0.336, ye = 0.173). The calculated CCT values for LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ are 2112 K and 6154 K, respectively. The CCT value of NCs above 5000 K, indicate cool white light emission, suitable for indoor applications such as household lighting (Fig. 4c and e).

Color purity (CP) is crucial for assessing the quality of emitted light. CP is calculated using the equation [65]:

where (xs, ys) are the chromaticity coordinates of the produced phosphor at different concentrations, (xi, yi) is set to (0.333, 0.333), and (xd, yd) indicates the coordinates of the intersection point between the two points and the upper boundary. The optimal CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu³⁺ NCs achieved a CP of 99.7 %, outperforming reported Eu3+-doped red phosphors such as CaMoO4:5Eu3+ (92 %) and Ca2GaNbO6:0.25Eu3+ (97 %) [66,67]. These findings confirm the material's suitability as a high CP red-emitting phosphor for display and lighting applications (Table 4).

The decay lifetimes of CDs, LCAO:7Eu3+, and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are analyzed using their decay curves at λexc i = 338 nm and λemi = 407 nm (Fig. 4f). The decay curves fit a double exponential function [68]:
(16)

Where I represent the emission intensity at time t, A1 and A2 are constants and τ1 and τ2 denote the double exponential components of the decay time. In luminous materials, the energy transfer phenomena frequently result in the double exponential decay behaviour. The following equation can be used to get the average lifetime (τavg):
(17)

The lifetimes for CDs, LCAO:7Eu3+, and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are 8.814 ns, 5.573 ns, and 4.883 ns, respectively. The shorter lifetimes for NCs indicate efficient energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ions via the FRET mechanism.

Internal quantum efficiency (IQE) is a key parameter for evaluating phosphors in w-LED applications. IQE is calculated using the formula [69]:
(18)
η =
∫
L s
∫
E R
− ∫
E s
where ER denotes the emission light spectrum of BaSO4, ES is the integrated intensity of the phosphor, LS represents the emission spectrum of phosphor, and η refers to the IQE value. The IQE values for LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are 77.33 % and 89.58 %, respectively (Fig. 4g). These values surpass previously reported phosphors (Table 5 ) [70–75], demonstrating excellent luminescence performance and potential for high-efficiency w-LED applications.

The stability of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu³⁺ NCs is crucial for practical applications in w-LEDs. To assess their optical stability, the samples are subjected to various conditions, including prolonged UV irradiation, thermal cycles, and pH variations. The results confirm the robustness of the composite material compared to standalone CDs. The PL spectra of CDs(4 wt%) and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs under UV exposure for different durations (0–300 min) are shown in Figs. S6a and b. Over time, the fluorescence intensity of CDs (4 wt%) decreased by 26 %, attributed to photo-bleaching, a process where prolonged UV exposure induces surface oxidation and quenches fluorescence [76]. In contrast, CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrated enhanced UV resistance, retaining 95 % of their initial PL intensity after 300 min of exposure. The 5 % reduction in intensity is attributed to limited photo-oxidation, wherein UV irradiation induces surface peroxide species formation, leading to mild surface oxidation and hydration of the internal LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix [77]. The protective role of the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix minimizes lattice degradation, preserving the material's luminescence.

Thermal durability is evaluated by subjecting CDs(4 wt%) and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs to ten heating-cooling cycles, heating to 150 °C for 10 min followed by natural cooling to room temperature. PL intensity measurements after each cycle are shown in Figs. S6c and d. CDs (4 wt%) experienced an 8 % reduction in fluorescence intensity, whereas the NCs showed only a 3 % decrease, confirming superior thermal stability. The LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix acts as a protective scaffold, mitigating thermal stress and preserving the composite's optical properties.

The pH-dependent fluorescence of CDs(4 wt%) and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs is shown in Figs. S6e and f. For CDs (4 wt%), the maximum emission intensity at 407 nm occurs at neutral pH, with reduced fluorescence in acidic and basic conditions. Similarly, CDs@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs exhibit emission at 613 nm, maintaining greater stability across a wide pH range. The composite's superior luminescence stability under neutral and variable pH conditions highlights its potential for pH sensing applications in biological and environmental systems. The remarkable stability of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs against UV irradiation, thermal cycles, and pH variations is attributed to the protective influence of the LCAO:7Eu3+ matrix. This matrix acts as a physical and chemical barrier, reducing oxidative and thermal degradation and enhancing the material's resilience. These characteristics make the composite an excellent candidate for applications requiring stable luminescence, such as w-LEDs, biological imaging, and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature dependent PL spectra (TDPL) and optical thermometry studies
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal quenching behavior of LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs is explained using the configurational coordinate diagram (Fig. 5f). Upon 395 nm excitation, electrons are elevated from the 7F0 ground state to the 5L6 excited state and subsequently relax to the 5D0 level through non-radiative (NR) processes (process a). At room temperature, these electrons return to the ground state via radiative transitions, producing luminescence. As temperature increases, phonon coupling enables electrons to overcome the activation energy (Ea), transitioning to the charge transfer band (CTB) (process b). These electrons return to the ground state via additional NR processes (processes c and f), resulting in reduced luminescence intensity. A higher Ea reduces the likelihood of thermal quenching, enhancing thermal stability. Anomalous thermal quenching (ATQ) may occur when electrons are trapped (process d) instead of transitioning directly to the ground state. At elevated temperatures, these trapped electrons are thermally re-excited to the 5D0 level (process e), temporarily increasing emission intensity. As temperature rises further, most trapped electrons return to the 5D0 state, and ATQ effects diminish.

Thermal quenching is quantitatively analyzed using an Arrhenius equation [83]:
(19)
I T
=
I 0
1 + A e x p
(
− Δ E
k B
T
)

The PL intensity at a specific temperature T (K), represented as IT, and the PL intensity at room temperature (RT), represented as I0, are both included in the calculation. The activation energy is represented as ΔE, and the Boltzmann constant as kB (8.617 × 10−5 eV/K), with A being an arbitrary constant. The relationship between ln[(I0/I)-1] and 1/(kBT) is plotted for LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 5g and h). The linear fit yielded Ea values of 0.27 eV and 0.39 eV for LCAO:7Eu3+ NPs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs, respectively. These values exceed those reported for many phosphors (Table 7 ) [34,84–87], indicating the superior resistance of the composite material to thermal quenching. The high Ea suggests that more energy is required for electrons to overcome the barrier, ensuring better thermal stability. The exceptional thermal stability and high activation energy of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs make them ideal for applications in high-performance w-LEDs. Their ability to maintain luminescence intensity under elevated temperatures and their resistance to thermal quenching outclass many commercial and reported phosphors. These features highlight their potential for robust and efficient lighting solutions in a wide range of temperature conditions.

The CIE color coordinates of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are determined across temperatures ranging from 300 K to 480 K, as shown in Fig. 6 a. At 420 K, the CIE coordinates are calculated to be (x, y) = (0.695, 0.305). With increasing temperature, CIE x exhibited a decreasing trend, while CIE y increased slightly, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. The shifts in CIE coordinates, Δx = 0.00275 and Δy = 0.00294, demonstrate minimal change, highlighting the excellent thermal stability of the material. The chromaticity shift (ΔS) is calculated using the equation [88]:
(20)

represent the chromaticity coordinates in the uniform color space u' v', while x and y denote the CIE coordinates within the chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931). Where 0 and t denote the chromaticity coordinates at temperatures of 300 K and 480 K, respectively. The computed ΔS value at 480 K is 0.0285, indicating minimal chromaticity shift and excellent color consistency under thermal conditions. The CIE, CCT, and CP values at various temperatures are summarized in Table S3.

The temperature-dependent emission intensity suggests potential use in fluorescence intensity thermometry, a non-invasive optical method for temperature measurement. This technique is highly relevant for biological tags, environmental monitoring, and inaccessible object temperature assessments. The fluorescence intensity ratio (FIR) of the peaks at 613 nm and 591 nm is calculated using [89]:
(20)
FIR =

In the equation, I1 stands for the integrated peak intensity of 613 nm, I2 represents the integrated peak intensity of 591 nm, kB stands for the Boltzmann constant, and B denotes the offset parameter. Fig. 6c and d shows the temperature-dependent FIR and the natural logarithm of FIR, ln(FIR) as a function of 1/kBT. Linear fitting yielded ΔE values of 0.26 eV for LCAO:7Eu3+ and 0.35 eV for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs, indicating superior thermal stability of the composite.

The absolute sensitivity (SA) and relative sensitivity (SR) are evaluated using the formulas [90]:
(21)

The sensitivity curves for LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs are depicted in Fig. 6e and f, respectively. The SA increased with temperature, reaching peak values of SA = 0.0042 K-1 = for LCAO:7Eu3+ and SA = 0.0047 K-1 for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ at 480 K. Conversely, SR exhibited a decreasing trend with rising temperature, achieving maximum values of SR = 2.8 % K−1 for LCAO:7Eu3+ and SR = 3.10 % K−1 for CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ at 300 K. The achieved sensitivity values (SA and SR) are compared with previously reported optical thermometry materials in Table 8 [91–95]. The CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrate outstanding performance in both sensitivity parameters, making them highly promising for precise and efficient optical thermometry applications.

The potential of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs as a red component for w-LEDs is evaluated by fabricating a w-LED using the synthesized bright red NCs combined with commercial blue and green phosphors. The electroluminescence (EL) spectrum, CIE chromaticity coordinates, and correlated color temperature (CCT) are shown in Fig. 7 . The inset in Fig. 7b present the good luminescent effect of the w-LED at 300 mA currents and 3.2 V voltages. The EL spectrum (Fig. 7a) demonstrates sharp emission peaks centered at 613 nm, 650 nm, and 692 nm, consistent with the earlier-obtained emission spectrum. The fabricated w-LED exhibits CIE chromaticity coordinates of (0.324,0.320), closely approaching the standard white isoenergetic point of (0.333,0.333). Furthermore, the CCT value of the w-LED is found to be 5939 K, indicating cool white light emission suitable for diverse applications. The comparative CRI analysis (Fig. 7c) revealed a superior color rendering index (Ra = 92, R9 = 91) for the fabricated w-LED, outperforming commercial w-LEDs (Ra = 79, R9 = 15). The EL spectra under varying forward bias currents (30–300 mA) are shown in Fig. 7d. The emission pattern and peak positions remain stable, with only an increase in intensity, further confirming the NCs' reliability and suitability for w-LED applications. Detailed data on CIE coordinates and Ra at different currents are provided in Table S4.

To show that the w-LED can improve plant development, its efficacy is evaluated in indoor plant cultivation. w-LED made using CDs (4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs and an n-UV 365 nm LED chip. In order to maximize photosynthesis, the produced w-LED probably generates enough blue and red light to overlap with the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll because it emits white light, which is a mixture of wavelengths including blue, green, and red. Using the chilli plant as an example, the outcomes of plant growth studies are compared by adjusting the experimental circumstances. First, two plastic pots filled with soil are used to hold two healthy chilli seedlings that had each germinated independently under sunlight and w-LED light. Throughout the research, same amounts of water and manure will be added to containers 1 and 2. To support photosynthesis throughout the day, plants in containers 1 and 2 receive 12–16 h of sunlight and w-LED light, respectively. The plant in container 1 uses the glucose that is saved during the day through photosynthesis as fuel at night. This energy promotes the growth and upkeep of cells. After 12–16 h of exposure to w-LED light, the plant in container 2 is subsequently kept in the dark. The lack of light facilitates the plant's energy allocation for cell elongation and division.

The CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrate immense potential as a luminescent material for anti-counterfeiting (AC) applications, providing secure and efficient information encryption and decryption capabilities. Under UV light at 365 nm, the material exhibits a vivid red luminescence, enabling the creation of patterns or labels invisible under ambient light but clearly visible under UV light, making it highly suitable for secure applications [98]. The viability of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs for AC applications is evaluated through screen printing and brush techniques to create fluorescent patterns on various substrates, including invitation cards, marble, newspaper, butter paper, leaves, ceramic, and cardboard. These patterns are invisible under daylight but displayed bright red luminescence when exposed to UV light.

Fig. 9 practically demonstrates a comparative analysis of luminescent screen-printed patterns the butterfly and apple designs on identical substrates, using two different luminescent materials such as LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+. The substrates used include an invitation card, cardboard sheet, marble, newspaper, and butter paper, ensuring a consistent testing environment for both materials. The butterfly and apple patterns are uniformly printed on the same substrates for both inks, allowing for a direct comparison of luminescence, brightness, and clarity. This design choice eliminates variability from the pattern itself and ensures that any differences observed are due solely to the luminescent properties of the materials. LCAO:7Eu3+ ink exhibited a reddish-orange glow under UV light, with moderate brightness and varying clarity depending on the substrate texture. Smooth surfaces, such as invitation cards and butter paper, provided better pattern clarity, while uneven surfaces like cardboard and marble diminished the luminescence intensity. In contrast, CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ ink significantly enhanced brightness and sharpness, producing vivid red patterns with superior fluorescence across all substrates. The incorporation of CDs improved fluorescence intensity and energy transfer efficiency, making it ideal for AC applications.

The AC patterns created using CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu³⁺ NCs demonstrated excellent photo-stability and resilience under challenging conditions. Patterns printed on OHP sheets maintained nearly constant luminescence intensity even after 120 min of continuous UV exposure (Fig. 10 a). While patterns subjected to temperatures ranging from 50 °C to 350 °C retained their integrity up to 250 °C, with negligible changes in luminescence intensity (Fig. 10b). Additionally, patterns treated with solvents of varying pH (acidic, neutral, and basic) showed consistent brightness and uniform luminescence, emphasizing their robustness under different chemical environments (Fig. 10c). Patterns on craft paper remained visually and luminescently stable over 180 days of storage at room temperature (Fig. 10d). This robust and consistent red emission highlights the environmental resilience of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+- based luminescent ink, affirming its suitability for secure applications. These results provide compelling evidence of the effectiveness and durability of the CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ ink in security applications.

Fig. 11 provides a comprehensive representation of luminescent properties and the diverse applications of materials in creating handwritten AC patterns and their stability under various environmental conditions. Fig. 11a displays the AC patterns developed using two security inks like LCAO:7Eu3+ and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+. Of two images, the AC pattern created using CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ security ink exhibits the most strong luminescence emission, which when exposed to UV light, emits a bright red hue visible to the human eye. The versatility of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs is further showcased by creating handwritten AC patterns on diverse substrates, including leaf, ceramic, glass, aluminum foil, and butter paper, using a simple brush application. Under UV light (365 nm), the patterns emitted sharp and distinct red luminescence, as shown in Fig. 11b–g. The durability of these patterns is validated by various testing. Patterns remained stable for up to 60 min of UV exposure before showing a slight decrease in intensity (Fig. 11h). Further, patterns preserved on petri dishes retained homogeneity and luminescence over 180 days of storage (Fig. 11i). AC pattern on marble substrates maintained brightness up to 150 °C, with a gradual decrease at higher temperatures (Fig. 11j). Luminescence remained stable in AC pattern drawn on steel plate for up to 30 min of water immersion, with slight degradation thereafter (Fig. 11k). The enhanced luminescence, excellent stability, and environmental resilience of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs make them ideal for encoding sensitive data with covert security features. These materials can be employed in document encryption, secure packaging, and counterfeit prevention. The enhanced brightness and clarity of the patterns, coupled with their adaptability to various substrates and environmental conditions, demonstrate the exceptional utility of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs as a next-generation security ink.

The study employs innovative multiple encryption strategies to bolster anti-counterfeiting measures using fluorescent CDs and CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs. These strategies leverage the distinct luminescence properties of the materials under UV light for secure and effective information encoding.

Binary Code Encryption: A robust binary encoding mechanism is demonstrated using the dual luminescent materials. Fig. 12 a illustrates the binary codes generated by combining the blue fluorescence of CDs with the red luminescence of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+. The decryption process involves three steps:
1.
Observation under UV light: The binary pattern is distinguished by identifying blue fluorescence and red luminescence.
2.
Assignment of binary values: Blue fluorescence is assigned the binary value “0,” while red luminescence corresponds to “1."
3.
Translation into characters: The binary sequence is decoded into alphanumeric characters, forming the word “ACHIEVE."

Under normal lighting conditions, the binary pattern is invisible, adding a layer of security by concealing the encoded information from casual observation. This simple yet effective method translates visible UV luminescence patterns into encrypted alphanumeric data, offering a versatile approach to anti-counterfeiting.

QR Code Integration: An advanced strategy incorporates high-precision Quick Response (QR) codes printed using CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs. As shown in Fig. 12b, the QR codes are crafted using screen printing and are designed to remain invisible under regular lighting conditions. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the QR codes become distinct and scannable. Decryption process involves,
1.
A smartphone equipped with a QR code reader or google lens scans the luminescent QR code.
2.
The smartphone retrieves the embedded information, such as images or text, stored within the code.

For instance, scanning the QR code in Fig. 12b reveals an image, demonstrating the material's ability to encode complex data securely. This feature ensures that the QR code is only visible under specific conditions, preventing unauthorized access or data leakage while maintaining the document's or product's appearance under regular light.

Thus the multi-layered encryption strategies demonstrated with CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs offer significant advantages:
•
Enhanced security: Invisible patterns under ambient light prevent casual tampering or copying.
•
Instant verification: QR code scanning provides real-time authentication and retrieval of stored data.
•
Covert features: UV-exclusive visibility ensures secure access to encoded information.
•
Versatile applications: These materials can be used in secure packaging, document authentication, and high-security labeling.

So the potential for integrating multi-color luminescent materials offers opportunities to expand storage capacity for encrypted data and diversify applications in secure storage, optical displays, and advanced anti-counterfeiting technologies. This work underscores the feasibility of utilizing luminescent NCs for secure and intelligent anti-counterfeiting solutions. The distinct luminescent behaviour under UV illumination and the ability to encode alphanumeric and QR code data highlight these materials potential for next-generation security technologies.

In this work, a novel and eco-friendly methodology is developed to enhance the luminescent performance of LCAO:7Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) by integrating them with CDs derived from natural sources. The resulting CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs demonstrated exceptional structural, optical, and functional properties. XRD studies confirmed the phase-pure tetragonal structure of LCAO:7Eu3+ with crystallite sizes ranging from 31.1 to 36.2 nm, while FE-SEM and TEM analyses revealed the successful incorporation of uniformly spherical CDs into the flaky LCAO matrix. Chemical analyses through XPS and FTIR indicated robust interactions between CDs and the LCAO:7Eu3+ host, contributing to significant improvements in luminescence. A remarkable 20-fold enhancement in photoluminescence (PL) intensity is achieved due to efficient FRET from CDs to Eu3+ ions. The NCs exhibited superior thermal stability, retaining 91.5 % of their luminescence at 420 K, with an activation energy of 0.39 eV, surpassing many reported phosphors. IQE of 89.58 % enabled the fabrication of cool w-LEDs with a CRI exceeding 90, demonstrating their viability for energy-efficient lighting solutions. The fabricated w-LEDs significantly enhanced indoor plant cultivation, promoting increased plant height(6.17 %), stem diameter (21.42 %), leaf width (10.65 %), and chlorophyll content (19.50 %), making them a sustainable alternative for controlled agriculture. Additionally, the NCs are successfully incorporated into anti-counterfeiting applications, creating advanced security labels with covert QR and binary codes visible only under UV light, ensuring high security and authenticity. This research highlights the multifunctional capabilities of CDs(4 wt%)@LCAO:7Eu3+ NCs, bridging advancements in energy-efficient lighting, sustainable agriculture, and optical security technologies. These findings lay the groundwork for future innovations in luminescent materials, with broad applications in diverse industrial and scientific domains.
